name: Current Affairs in Geography, Climate & Disasters (1)

sourceRefs.label: downtoearth.org.in/visionias.in - India's 2025 extreme weather statistics; Climate Risk Index 2025/2026 rankings

researchNotes: Second topic in the new Part A Current-Affairs rotation (Unit II's turn, per the Science→Geography→History→Polity→Economy→TN-Culture cycle). All facts are recent (2025-2026 reports) and explicitly time-stamped — these statistics will be superseded by next year's reports, so the figures should be understood as 'as reported in 2025/2026', not permanent facts.

reading.intro: Covers India's recent extreme-weather and climate-disaster statistics, drawn from 2025-2026 climate reports — directly relevant to the syllabus's 'Natural calamity - Disaster management' and 'Climate change' clauses, updated with the latest available figures.

qa.question: How frequently did India face extreme weather events in 2025, per recent reporting?
qa.answer: India faced extreme weather on 331 of 334 days in 2025 — extreme weather events occurred on 99% of days between 1 January and 30 November 2025.

qa.question: What was the human and economic toll of these 2025 extreme weather events?
qa.answer: At least 4,419 deaths; around 17.4 million hectares of cropped land affected; at least 181,459 houses destroyed; and approximately 77,189 animals killed.

qa.question: Where does India rank on the Climate Risk Index 2026, and what are the associated losses?
qa.answer: 9th among the most climate-affected countries, with approximately 80,000 deaths and USD 170 billion in losses attributed to climate impacts.

qa.question: Where did India rank on the Climate Risk Index 2025 (covering the longer period 1993-2022)?
qa.answer: 6th most affected country globally over that period, with losses exceeding USD 180 billion.

qa.question: How has India's average temperature changed in recent decades, and what specific record was set in February 2025?
qa.answer: The Indian landmass was warmer by nearly 0.9°C during 2015-2024 compared to the 1901-1930 baseline. The India Meteorological Department (IMD) confirmed February 2025 as the hottest February on record.

qa.question: What change has been observed in Himalayan glacial lakes, and what risk does this raise?
qa.answer: Glacial lakes and other water bodies in the Himalayan region saw a 10.81% increase in area from 2011 to 2024 due to climate change — raising the risk of catastrophic Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs).

qa.question: Approximately how many people in India have been internally displaced due to climate change, per the IDMC?
qa.answer: About 14 million people, per estimates from the Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC).
